questions.question: What is the main causative agent of bronchiolitis in infants?
questions.explanation: Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) is the most common cause of bronchiolitis in children under 2 years of age, responsible for up to 70% of cases.

questions.question: What histopathological finding characterizes RSV bronchiolitis?
questions.explanation: RSV causes necrosis of the bronchiolar epithelium with lymphocytic infiltration and obstruction by cellular debris, edema, and mucus, leading to air trapping.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for mild RSV bronchiolitis?
questions.explanation: The management of mild bronchiolitis is supportive: hydration, aspiration of secretions and oxygen if necessary. Corticosteroids, ribavirin or routine antibiotics are not recommended.

questions.question: What risk factor is associated with greater severity in RSV bronchiolitis?
questions.explanation: Prematurity is a well-established risk factor for severe RSV bronchiolitis due to lung and immune immaturity, along with congenital heart disease and bronchopulmonary dysplasia.

questions.question: What clinical sign is characteristic of RSV bronchiolitis?
questions.explanation: Expiratory wheezing is the characteristic sign of bronchiolitis, due to obstruction of the small airway by edema, mucus, and cellular debris.

questions.question: What prophylaxis is recommended for RSV in high-risk infants?
questions.explanation: Palivizumab is a monoclonal antibody indicated for seasonal prophylaxis in premature infants or infants with hemodynamically significant congenital heart disease to prevent severe RSV infection.

questions.question: What is the mechanism of action of oseltamivir?
questions.explanation: Oseltamivir is an inhibitor of viral neuraminidase, an enzyme essential for the release of viral particles from the infected cell, thus limiting the spread of the influenza virus.

questions.question: What is the optimal therapeutic window to start oseltamivir in influenza?
questions.explanation: Initiation of oseltamivir treatment is most effective when administered within the first 48 hours of symptom onset, reducing the duration and severity of the disease.

questions.question: In which group of patients is treatment with oseltamivir especially recommended?
questions.explanation: Oseltamivir is especially indicated in hospitalized patients with confirmed or suspected influenza, as well as in those with risk factors for complications, due to its benefit in reducing mortality.

questions.question: What is the standard dose of oseltamivir for an adult with normal kidney function?
questions.explanation: In adults with normal kidney function, the recommended dose of oseltamivir for the treatment of influenza is 75 mg every 12 hours for 5 days.

questions.question: What is a common gastrointestinal side effect of oseltamivir?
questions.explanation: Nausea and vomiting are the most common adverse effects of oseltamivir, occurring in approximately 10% of patients, generally mild and transient.

questions.question: What virus is NOT covered by oseltamivir?
questions.explanation: Oseltamivir is designed specifically against influenza A and B viruses, but has no antiviral activity against SARS-CoV-2, which causes COVID-19.

questions.question: What is the main rhinovirus serotype involved in the common cold?
questions.explanation: Rhinovirus A is the serotype most frequently isolated in upper respiratory tract infections, being responsible for most cases of the common cold.

questions.question: What cell receptor does rhinovirus use to infect the respiratory epithelium?
questions.explanation: The rhinovirus binds to the intercellular adhesion molecule 1 (ICAM-1) present in the respiratory epithelium, facilitating its entry and replication.

questions.question: What is the main route of transmission of rhinovirus in the common cold?
questions.explanation: Rhinovirus transmission occurs mainly through respiratory droplets expelled when coughing or sneezing, and by direct contact with contaminated surfaces.

questions.question: What is the treatment of choice for the common cold due to rhinovirus?
questions.explanation: The rhinovirus common cold is self-limiting and has no specific antiviral treatment; Management is based on symptomatic measures such as antipyretics and decongestants.

questions.question: What is the main factor that favors the spread of rhinovirus in temperate climates?
questions.explanation: Low temperatures favor the survival of rhinovirus in the environment and increase the susceptibility of the respiratory epithelium, increasing transmission.

questions.question: Which age group has the highest incidence of the common cold due to rhinovirus?
questions.explanation: Infants and young children have a higher incidence of the common cold due to rhinovirus due to the immaturity of their immune system and exposure in daycare.

questions.question: What is the main clinical sign of adenovirus conjunctivitis?
questions.explanation: The presence of lymphoid follicles in the tarsal conjunctiva is the characteristic finding of adenoviral follicular conjunctivitis, differentiating it from other infectious conjunctivitis.

questions.question: What type of sample is most suitable for the virological diagnosis of ocular adenovirus?
questions.explanation: Conjunctival exudate allows infected epithelial cells to be obtained for viral culture, immunofluorescence or PCR, being the sample of choice in adenoviral conjunctivitis.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for adenovirus follicular conjunctivitis?
questions.explanation: There is no specific approved antiviral therapy; Management is based on supportive measures such as ocular lubricants and cold compresses to relieve symptoms until spontaneous resolution.

questions.question: What late complication can appear after uncomplicated adenoviral conjunctivitis?
questions.explanation: Corneal subepithelial opacities are a typical late immune-mediated complication of epidemic adenovirus keratoconjunctivitis, which can persist for weeks or months.

questions.question: Which adenovirus serotype is most frequently associated with epidemic keratoconjunctivitis?
questions.explanation: Adenovirus serotype 8 is the most frequently implicated in outbreaks of epidemic keratoconjunctivitis, followed by serotypes 19, 37 and 4.

questions.question: What is the main route of nosocomial transmission of ocular adenovirus?
questions.explanation: Nosocomial transmission occurs mainly through contaminated ophthalmic instruments (tonometers, slit lamps) and staff hands, and strict hygiene is key to preventing outbreaks.

questions.question: What is the main viral cause of laryngotracheitis (croup) in children?
questions.explanation: Parainfluenza virus type 1 is the most common etiological agent of viral croup, especially in children from 6 months to 3 years.

questions.question: What clinical finding is characteristic of viral croup?
questions.explanation: Viral croup classically presents with inspiratory stridor, barking cough, and dysphonia, due to subglottic inflammation.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for mild to moderate croup?
questions.explanation: Oral or intramuscular dexamethasone reduces subglottic inflammation and is the treatment of choice in mild to moderate croup.

questions.question: What radiological sign is typical of viral croup on a neck x-ray?
questions.explanation: The anteroposterior neck x-ray shows pencil-point subglottic narrowing, characteristic of viral croup.

questions.question: What is the age group most affected by parainfluenza laryngotracheitis?
questions.explanation: Viral croup typically affects children between 6 months and 3 years, with peak incidence around age 2 years.

questions.question: What pathophysiological mechanism underlies stridor in viral croup?
questions.explanation: Inflammation and edema of the subglottic mucosa, which is narrow in children, reduces the airway lumen and generates inspiratory stridor.

questions.question: What is the main clinical manifestation of metapneumovirus in bone marrow transplant recipients?
questions.explanation: In immunocompromised patients, especially bone marrow transplant recipients, human metapneumovirus frequently causes severe pneumonia with high morbidity.

questions.question: Which diagnostic method has the greatest sensitivity to detect metapneumovirus in immunocompromised patients?
questions.explanation: Real-time PCR is the most sensitive technique for the detection of human metapneumovirus, especially in immunocompromised patients with low viral load.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for metapneumovirus infection in lung transplant recipients?
questions.explanation: Inhaled ribavirin has been used as a first-line treatment in metapneumovirus infections in lung transplant recipients, although with limited evidence.

questions.question: What complication is most common in children with acute lymphoblastic leukemia infected by metapneumovirus?
questions.explanation: In children immunocompromised by leukemia, metapneumovirus infection frequently progresses to severe respiratory failure due to viral pneumonitis.

questions.question: What is the main route of transmission of metapneumovirus in transplant units?
questions.explanation: Metapneumovirus is transmitted primarily by respiratory droplets, requiring contact and respiratory isolation in immunocompromised patients.

questions.question: What histopathological finding characterizes metapneumovirus pneumonia in autopsies of immunocompromised patients?
questions.explanation: Metapneumovirus pneumonia shows multinucleated giant cells and syncytial epithelial cells in the lung tissue, similar to respiratory syncytial virus.

questions.question: What is the main route of transmission of SARS-CoV-2?
questions.explanation: The transmission of SARS-CoV-2 occurs mainly through respiratory droplets expelled when coughing, sneezing or talking, making it a highly contagious pathogen in closed spaces.

questions.question: Which SARS-CoV-2 protein binds to the ACE2 receptor?
questions.explanation: The S (spike) protein is responsible for binding to the ACE2 receptor in human cells, facilitating the entry of the virus and triggering infection.

questions.question: What is the first-line antiviral treatment for mild COVID-19 in high-risk patients?
questions.explanation: Nirmatrelvir/ritonavir is the recommended oral antiviral for patients with mild to moderate COVID-19 and high risk of progression, significantly reducing hospitalization and death.

questions.question: What is a characteristic finding of SARS-CoV-2 pneumonia on computed tomography?
questions.explanation: Ground glass opacities, frequently bilateral and peripheral, are the most common tomographic finding in SARS-CoV-2 pneumonia, reflecting alveolar inflammation.

questions.question: What pathophysiological mechanism explains the cytokine storm in severe COVID-19?
questions.explanation: The cytokine storm in severe COVID-19 is due to excessive activation of macrophages and massive release of proinflammatory cytokines such as IL-6 and TNF-α, causing systemic tissue damage.

questions.question: What is the main seasonal coronavirus that causes the common cold in adults?
questions.explanation: HCoV-229E is one of the seasonal coronaviruses that typically causes mild upper respiratory tract infections, such as the common cold, in immunocompetent adults.

questions.question: What is the most characteristic radiological finding of SARS-CoV-2 pneumonia?
questions.explanation: SARS-CoV-2 pneumonia typically presents with bilateral ground-glass opacities on CT scan, unlike the lobar consolidation typical of bacterial pneumonias.

questions.question: Which respiratory virus is most frequently associated with pneumonia in immunocompromised patients?
questions.explanation: Cytomegalovirus is a common cause of viral pneumonitis in immunocompromised patients, especially after solid organ or bone marrow transplantation, unlike the other viruses that are more common in immunocompetent patients.

questions.question: What is the test of choice for the diagnosis of influenza A virus pneumonia?
questions.explanation: Real-time PCR of nasopharyngeal swab is the gold standard for the diagnosis of influenza A, due to its high sensitivity and specificity, surpassing culture and serology.

questions.question: Which of the following clinical findings is more suggestive of viral than bacterial pneumonia?
questions.explanation: Persistent dry cough is characteristic of viral pneumonia, while purulent expectoration, pleuritic pain, and sudden fever are more typical of bacterial pneumonia.

questions.question: What antiviral treatment is indicated in severe pneumonia due to confirmed influenza A virus?
questions.explanation: Oseltamivir is the antiviral of choice for influenza A, especially in severe or high-risk cases, administered within 48 hours of symptom onset to reduce mortality.

questions.question: Which of the following viruses is associated with interstitial pneumonia with severe hypoxemia in young children?
questions.explanation: Respiratory syncytial virus is the main cause of bronchiolitis and viral pneumonia in infants, and can cause severe hypoxemia and require hospitalization, more so than the other viruses listed.

questions.question: What main advantage does multiplex PCR in a respiratory panel offer over conventional methods?
questions.explanation: Multiplex PCR allows the detection of several respiratory viruses and bacteria in a single reaction, accelerating etiological diagnosis and improving precision compared to methods that detect a single agent.

questions.question: What is a pathogen commonly included in a multiplex PCR respiratory panel?
questions.explanation: Human rhinovirus is one of the most common respiratory viruses and is included in most respiratory multiplex PCR panels, while the other pathogens are not typical of the upper respiratory tract.

questions.question: What type of sample is most suitable for performing a respiratory multiplex PCR?
questions.explanation: The nasopharyngeal swab is the sample of choice because it contains respiratory epithelial cells where viruses replicate, guaranteeing high sensitivity for detection by PCR.

questions.question: In which clinical scenario is it most indicated to request a multiplex PCR respiratory panel?
options: Patient with recurrent urinary tract infection
Patient with chronic headache without fever
questions.explanation: In immunocompromised patients, rapid identification of the respiratory agent by multiplex PCR allows antiviral or antibiotic treatment to be directed and complications to be avoided.

questions.question: What is an important limitation of multiplex PCR in respiratory panel?
options: Does not detect respiratory syncytial virus
Does not distinguish active infection from colonization
questions.explanation: Multiplex PCR detects genetic material from the pathogen, but does not differentiate whether it is active infection or asymptomatic colonization, which can lead to overdiagnosis.

questions.question: What microorganism can cause false negatives in multiplex PCR due to genetic variability?
questions.explanation: The influenza A virus, especially subtypes such as H3N2, suffers from frequent mutations that can cause PCR primers to fail, generating false negatives.

questions.question: In which group of patients with RSV is ribavirin indicated?
questions.explanation: Ribavirin is indicated primarily in severely immunocompromised patients with RSV infection, as they have a higher risk of severe disease and mortality. In healthy patients it is not routinely recommended.

questions.question: What is the route of administration of ribavirin for RSV?
questions.explanation: Ribavirin can be administered by inhalation (most studied form in RSV) or intravenously in certain contexts, such as in immunocompromised patients. The choice depends on the severity and condition of the patient.

questions.question: What adverse effect limits the use of inhaled ribavirin?
questions.explanation: Acute bronchospasm is a relevant adverse effect of inhaled ribavirin, which may occur during administration, especially in patients with bronchial hyperresponsiveness. Requires careful monitoring.

questions.question: What is the main indication for ribavirin in neonatal RSV?
questions.explanation: In neonates, ribavirin is indicated only in cases of confirmed severe RSV infection, such as those with chronic lung disease or congenital heart disease. It is not used for routine prevention or prophylaxis.

questions.question: What condition contraindicates the use of inhaled ribavirin?
questions.explanation: Active bronchial asthma is an important relative contraindication to inhaled ribavirin, due to the high risk of severe bronchospasm. In these patients, the intravenous route is preferred if treatment is indicated.

questions.question: Which pediatric population with RSV benefits most from ribavirin?
questions.explanation: Children with bone marrow transplants (immunocompromised) are at greater risk of severe RSV pneumonia and mortality, and therefore benefit the most from ribavirin treatment. In mild patients it is not recommended.

questions.question: What is the mechanism of action of palivizumab?
questions.explanation: Palivizumab is a humanized monoclonal antibody that targets the F protein of the respiratory syncytial virus (RSV), neutralizing the virus and preventing infection.

questions.question: In which population is palivizumab indicated?
questions.explanation: Palivizumab is indicated in high-risk infants, such as extremely premature infants, with bronchopulmonary dysplasia or hemodynamically significant congenital heart disease, to prevent serious RSV infection.

questions.question: How often is palivizumab given during RSV season?
questions.explanation: Palivizumab is administered intramuscularly every 4 weeks during the RSV circulation season, generally from November to March in the Northern Hemisphere, completing up to 5 doses.

questions.question: Which virus is the primary target for palivizumab prophylaxis?
questions.explanation: Palivizumab is designed specifically against respiratory syncytial virus (RSV), the leading cause of severe bronchiolitis in infants, and has no activity against other respiratory viruses.

questions.question: What is a contraindication for the use of palivizumab?
questions.explanation: Severe hypersensitivity to palivizumab or any of its excipients is an absolute contraindication; the other options are target populations or conditions where indicated.

questions.question: What type of immunoprophylaxis does palivizumab represent?
questions.explanation: Palivizumab provides passive immunoprophylaxis by administering preformed antibodies, unlike vaccines that stimulate endogenous antibody production (active immunization).

questions.question: What is the main pathophysiological mechanism of postviral myocarditis?
questions.explanation: Postviral myocarditis is caused by an excessive inflammatory response mediated by cytokines and cytotoxic T lymphocytes that damage myocytes, not by coronary obstruction or valvular degeneration.

questions.question: What infectious agent is most frequently associated with postviral myocarditis?
questions.explanation: Coxsackievirus group B, an enterovirus, is the agent most frequently implicated in viral myocarditis, especially in children and young adults.

questions.question: What is the typical first clinical manifestation of postviral Guillain-Barré syndrome?
options: Ascending and symmetrical weakness of extremities
questions.explanation: Guillain-Barré syndrome is characterized by an acute demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy that begins with ascending and symmetrical weakness of the extremities, often after a viral infection.

questions.question: Which cerebrospinal fluid finding is characteristic of Guillain-Barré syndrome?
questions.explanation: Albuminocytological dissociation (elevated proteins with normal cell count) is the typical finding in the CSF of Guillain-Barré syndrome, differentiating it from infectious meningitis.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for postviral myocarditis with ventricular dysfunction?
questions.explanation: Treatment of postviral myocarditis focuses on hemodynamic support with beta blockers, ACEIs, and diuretics; Immunoglobulin and corticosteroids have not shown consistent benefit in clinical trials.

questions.question: What previous viral infection is most reported as a trigger for Guillain-Barré syndrome?
questions.explanation: Although it is not viral, Campylobacter jejuni infection is the most frequently associated with Guillain-Barré syndrome, triggering autoimmunity through molecular mimicry with gangliosides.
